lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: My necessaries are embark’d: farewell:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And, sister, as the winds give benefit

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And convoy is assistant, do not sleep,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: But let me hear from you.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Do you doubt that?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: For Hamlet and the trifling of his favour,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Hold it a fashion and a toy in blood,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: A violet in the youth of primy nature,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Forward, not permanent, sweet, not lasting,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The perfume and suppliance of a minute;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: No more.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: No more but so?

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Think it no more:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: For nature, crescent, does not grow alone

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: In thews and bulk, but, as this temple waxes,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The inward service of the mind and soul

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Grows wide withal. Perhaps he loves you now,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And now no soil nor cautel doth besmirch

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The virtue of his will: but you must fear,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: His greatness weigh’d, his will is not his own;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: For he himself is subject to his birth:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: He may not, as unvalued persons do,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Carve for himself; for on his choice depends

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The safety and health of this whole state;

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And therefore must his choice be circumscribed

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Unto the voice and yielding of that body

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Whereof he is the head. Then if he says he loves you,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: It fits your wisdom so far to believe it

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: As he in his particular act and place

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: May give his saying deed; which is no further

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Than the main voice of Denmark goes withal.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Then weigh what loss your honour may sustain,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: If with too credent ear you list his songs,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Or lose your heart, or your chaste treasure open

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: To his unmaster’d importunity.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Fear it, Ophelia, fear it, my dear sister,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And keep you in the rear of your affection,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Out of the shot and danger of desire.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The chariest maid is prodigal enough,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: If she unmask her beauty to the moon:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Virtue itself ’scapes not calumnious strokes:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: The canker galls the infants of the spring,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Too oft before their buttons be disclosed,

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: And in the morn and liquid dew of youth

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Contagious blastments are most imminent.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Be wary then; best safety lies in fear:

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Youth to itself rebels, though none else near.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I shall the effect of this good lesson keep,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: As watchman to my heart. But, good my brother,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Do not, as some ungracious pastors do,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Show me the steep and thorny way to heaven;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Whiles, like a puff’d and reckless libertine,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Himself the primrose path of dalliance treads,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And recks not his own rede.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: O, fear me not.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: I stay too long: but here my father comes.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Yet here, Laertes! aboard, aboard, for shame!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The wind sits in the shoulder of your sail,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And you are stay’d for. There; my blessing with thee!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And these few precepts in thy memory

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: See thou character. Give thy thoughts no tongue,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Nor any unproportion’d thought his act.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Be thou familiar, but by no means vulgar.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Those friends thou hast, and their adoption tried,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Grapple them to thy soul with hoops of steel;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: But do not dull thy palm with entertainment

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Of each new-hatch’d, unfledged comrade. Beware

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Of entrance to a quarrel, but being in,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Bear’t that the opposed may beware of thee.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Give every man thy ear, but few thy voice;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Take each man’s censure, but reserve thy judgment.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Costly thy habit as thy purse can buy,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: But not express’d in fancy; rich, not gaudy;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: For the apparel oft proclaims the man,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And they in France of the best rank and station

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Are of a most select and generous chief in that.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Neither a borrower nor a lender be;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: For loan oft loses both itself and friend,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And borrowing dulls the edge of husbandry.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: This above all: to thine ownself be true,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And it must follow, as the night the day,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Thou canst not then be false to any man.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Farewell: my blessing season this in thee!

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Most humbly do I take my leave, my lord.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The time invites you; go; your servants tend.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Farewell, Ophelia; and remember well

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: What I have said to you.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: ’Tis in my memory lock’d,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And you yourself shall keep the key of it.

lines.speaker: LAERTES
lines.text: Farewell.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What is’t, Ophelia, he hath said to you?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: So please you, something touching the Lord Hamlet.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Marry, well bethought:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: ’Tis told me, he hath very oft of late

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Given private time to you; and you yourself

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Have of your audience been most free and bounteous:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: If it be so, as so ’tis put on me,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And that in way of caution, I must tell you,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: You do not understand yourself so clearly

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As it behoves my daughter and your honour.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What is between you? give me up the truth.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: He hath, my lord, of late made many tenders

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Of his affection to me.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Affection! pooh! you speak like a green girl,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Unsifted in such perilous circumstance.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Do you believe his tenders, as you call them?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I do not know, my lord, what I should think.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Marry, I’ll teach you: think yourself a baby;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That you have ta’en these tenders for true pay,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Which are not sterling. Tender yourself more dearly;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Or—not to crack the wind of the poor phrase,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Running it thus—you’ll tender me a fool.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: My lord, he hath importuned me with love

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: In honourable fashion.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Ay, fashion you may call it; go to, go to.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And hath given countenance to his speech, my lord,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: With almost all the holy vows of heaven.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Ay, springes to catch woodcocks. I do know,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: When the blood burns, how prodigal the soul

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Lends the tongue vows: these blazes, daughter,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Giving more light than heat, extinct in both,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Even in their promise, as it is a-making,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: You must not take for fire. From this time

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Be somewhat scanter of your maiden presence;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Set your entreatments at a higher rate

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Than a command to parley. For Lord Hamlet,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Believe so much in him, that he is young,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And with a larger tether may he walk

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Than may be given you: in few, Ophelia,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Do not believe his vows; for they are brokers,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Not of that dye which their investments show,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: But mere implorators of unholy suits,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Breathing like sanctified and pious bonds,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The better to beguile. This is for all:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I would not, in plain terms, from this time forth,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Have you so slander any moment leisure,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As to give words or talk with the Lord Hamlet.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Look to’t, I charge you: come your ways.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I shall obey, my lord.

stage_directions.text: A double blessing is a double grace; Occasion smiles upon a second leave.

cause this to take root (ripen) in you

present me with a foolish daughter (or: with a bastard child)

annotations.title: Laertes's lecture and the economy of female chastity
annotations.body: Laertes's warnings to Ophelia treat her virginity as a family asset subject to market risks: 'your chaste treasure,' the 'canker' that 'galls the infants of the spring.' The lecture is conventional Elizabethan doctrine — a brother's duty to police his sister's sexual conduct — but Shakespeare sharpens the ironies. Ophelia's reply, delivered with the cool grace of a younger sister who has heard the speech before, turns it back on him: 'show me the steep and thorny way to heaven / Whiles, like a puff'd and reckless libertine, / Himself the primrose path of dalliance treads.' She sees through the sermon's asymmetry. It is the last time in the play she will be permitted this much rhetorical authority over a man.
sources: Carol Thomas Neely, Broken Nuptials in Shakespeare's Plays (1985)

annotations.title: Polonius's precepts as verbal portraiture
annotations.body: Polonius's famous advice ('Neither a borrower nor a lender be,' 'to thine own self be true') has been quoted out of context so often that readers forget its dramatic function. The maxims are recycled commonplaces from late-Tudor conduct manuals; what Shakespeare does is stitch them end-to-end without connecting logic, producing the effect of a mind that values sententious phrasing above any coherent philosophy. Modern productions often play this speech for comedy; Elizabethan audiences heard a magistrate who has mistaken the cadences of wisdom for its substance. The final line — 'This above all: to thine own self be true' — is the phrase most liable to moral inversion, since the play will show Polonius's self to be precisely the thing one ought not to be true to.

annotations.title: Ophelia's 'I do not know, my lord, what I should think'
annotations.body: Polonius's interrogation of Ophelia about Hamlet's love-tokens is the scene's emotional crux. Ophelia begins with guarded confidence ('He hath, my lord, of late made many tenders') and ends with obedience so complete it erases her own perception: 'I shall obey, my lord.' Between these poles Polonius pries open a private experience she can barely articulate and reframes it as a family political problem. The line 'Tender yourself more dearly' puns on 'tender' as a coin, and its financial register makes clear that Ophelia's love is being appraised as capital. Janet Adelman observes that Ophelia's tragedy begins here, not with Hamlet's cruelty — she loses, in this scene, the language to think her own experience.

annotations.title: 'Springes to catch woodcocks'
annotations.body: Polonius's phrase describing Hamlet's vows to Ophelia compares them to snares set for woodcocks — birds so proverbially stupid that they walk into the same trap twice. The image reduces Hamlet's love to predation and Ophelia to a dim bird. The spring-trap (a springe) was a familiar rural device, triggered by the victim's own footstep; Polonius's metaphor thus blames Ophelia for her susceptibility while pretending only to warn her. This is the scene's quiet cruelty: the father speaks the language of protection and performs the work of a cynic. Shakespeare's hunting imagery recurs throughout the play — the 'hawk from a handsaw,' the 'galled jade,' the pirate-ship ambush — and always accompanies characters who are being trapped or trapping.

trials.prompt: What does Laertes warn Ophelia about before leaving for France?
options: The political danger of associating with Prince Hamlet.
Hamlet's affections toward her, which he calls passing and unreliable.
The rumor that Polonius intends to send her to a convent.
That the King has designs of his own on her.

trials.prompt: When Ophelia tells Laertes to show her the way to heaven while he himself treads the 'primrose path of dalliance,' what is she doing?
options: Accepting his advice and resolving to live more chastely.
Pointing out the hypocrisy of a brother who polices her conduct while indulging his own.
Mocking him as a hypocritical Puritan.
Confessing that she has already compromised her virtue.

trials.prompt: Which of these is NOT a precept Polonius delivers to Laertes in this scene?
options: 'Give every man thy ear, but few thy voice.'
'Neither a borrower nor a lender be.'
'This above all: to thine own self be true.'
'Trust not a friend who praises you too much.'

trials.prompt: What does Polonius's phrase 'springes to catch woodcocks' imply about Hamlet's expressions of love?
options: That they are genuine declarations suitable for marriage.
That they are traps set for a gullible victim.
That they are harmless jests among courtiers.
That they are Latin exercises Hamlet is practicing.

trials.prompt: What does Polonius order Ophelia to do at the end of the scene?
options: Return all of Hamlet's letters and refuse to see him.
Report any meetings with Hamlet directly to the King.
Stop speaking with Hamlet and not give him any of her time.
Accept Hamlet's proposals but on her father's terms.
